message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN. Double-check before saving.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[zugzwang]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path and the word count.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: Rook Endgames: Lucena, Philidor, the 7th Rank and Rook Activity
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: The two pillars: LUCENA position (building a bridge to promote R+P vs R) and PHILIDOR position (passive 3rd-rank defense then check from behind). Give canonical positions and method for each. Add rooks belong behind passed pawns and activity over material.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.content:
# Rook Endgames: Lucena, Philidor, the 7th Rank and Rook Activity

> The two foundational rook-and-pawn techniques (Lucena to win, Philidor to draw) plus the guiding principles of rook activity: rooks belong behind passed pawns, and activity routinely beats material.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2200
**Prerequisites:** [King and Pawn Endgames](king-pawn-endgames.md), [Opposition](opposition.md)

## The idea

Rook endgames are the most common endgame in practical chess, so two positions are non-negotiable knowledge:

- **Lucena** — the winning method for the stronger side in R+P vs R, when your king already shelters in front of its pawn. You win by **"building a bridge"**: using your rook to block the enemy's checks so your king can escape.
- **Philidor** — the drawing method for the weaker side. While the attacker's king has **not** crossed the line of your pawn, you keep your rook passive on your **third rank**; the moment the pawn advances to that rank, you swing behind it and **check from the rear**.

Layered on top are two principles that decide most rook endings: **rooks belong behind passed pawns** (yours and the opponent's), and an **active rook is worth roughly a pawn** — so trade material for activity, not the reverse.

## Why it matters

R+P vs R arises constantly from simplified middlegames. Knowing whether a position is "Lucena-winning" or "Philidor-drawn" tells you what to aim for from far away — which pawns to trade, which file to seize, whether to head for the position at all. Players who *don't* know these techniques routinely throw away won games and lose drawn ones. The activity principle then governs everything else: in rook endings a passive rook is a liability worth more than a pawn.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . r . .
2 . . . . . P . .
1 . . . . . K . .
  a b c d e f g h
```

*Philidor: Black's rook holds the third rank while White's king is shut out behind the pawn.*
**FEN:** 8/7k/8/8/8/5r2/5P2/5K2 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White. (Schematic Philidor third-rank setup.)
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/7k/8/8/8/5r2/5P2/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)

Black keeps the rook on the 3rd rank (e.g. **...Rf3, ...Ra3**) so the White king cannot advance to the 6th. White cannot make progress while the rook patrols that rank: if **f3** (pawn reaches the 3rd), Black drops the rook to the **1st rank** (**...Ra1+, ...Rh1**) and checks the king from behind, since it now has no pawn shelter.

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . R . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . r .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```

*Bridge-building idea: the rook on the 4th–5th rank will interpose to block checks.*
**FEN:** 7k/8/8/3R4/8/6r1/8/4K3 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White. (Lucena bridge motif — rook ready to shield the king.)
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/3R4/8/6r1/8/4K3_w_-_-_0_1)

In the full Lucena (see Worked example 1), the defending rook checks the advancing king from the side. White's rook drops to its **4th rank** and, at the critical moment, **interposes** to block the check — the "bridge" — letting the king walk out and the pawn queen.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Lucena (winning):** stronger king in front of pawn, pawn on the 7th, build a bridge. Works for rook-pawns-excepted (a- and h-pawns need separate, often drawn, treatment).
- **Philidor (drawing):** passive 3rd-rank defense, then rear checks once the pawn advances. The defender's king sits in front of the pawn.
- **Back-rank / short-side / long-side defense:** when the defender's king is cut off, check from the **long side** (more files between rook and king) and keep your king on the **short side**.
- **Rook behind the passed pawn:** the attacker's pawn grows stronger as it advances; the defender's rook grows stronger.
- **The 7th rank (Tarrasch rule's cousin):** a rook on the 7th attacks pawns and confines the king — often worth more than a pawn.
- **Active vs passive rook:** the deciding factor in the majority of practical R-endings.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[R plus P vs R] --> B{My king in front of pawn}
  B -->|Yes attacker| C{Pawn on 7th and king sheltered}
  C -->|Yes| D[Build a bridge Lucena win]
  C -->|No| E[Advance king and pawn first]
  B -->|Yes defender| F{Attacker king crossed pawn line}
  F -->|No| G[Hold 3rd rank Philidor]
  F -->|Yes| H[Check from behind or long side]
  A --> I{Loose pawns on a rank}
  I -->|Yes| J[Seize the 7th rank]
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Lucena, building the bridge

```
8 . . K k . . . .
7 . . P . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . r .
1 . . R . . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```

*Canonical Lucena: White to play and win by building a bridge.*
**FEN:** 2Kk4/2P5/8/8/8/8/6r1/2R5 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/2Kk4/2P5/8/8/8/8/6r1/2R5_w_-_-_0_1)

**Solution:** First make luft for the king. **1.Rc1-c4!** (the rook reaches its 4th rank, ready to shield). Now **1...Rg1** (or any rook move) **2.Kc8-d7+? ** is premature; instead **2.Kb7** — wait, the clean method: with the rook already on c4, White plays **2.Kb7** to free c8, then on checks from the side the king walks toward the rook: **...Rb2+ 3.Ka6 Rc2** (cutting) is met by **4.Rc5!** and **5.Rc... ** the rook interposes on the 4th/5th rank to block the check. The king escapes via b6–c6 and the c-pawn queens. The single idea to remember: **rook to the 4th rank, king walks out toward the rook, rook interposes** to break the checking chain.

### Example 2 — Rook behind the passed pawn

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . k .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 P . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . K .
1 R . . . . . r .
  a b c d e f g h
```

*White's rook behind its own a-pawn; the pawn marches and the rook supports from a1.*
**FEN:** 8/8/6k1/8/P7/8/6K1/R5r1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/6k1/8/P7/8/6K1/R5r1_w_-_-_0_1)

**Solution:** The rook on **a1** is perfectly placed behind its passer. White pushes **a5–a6–a7**, the rook supporting from the rear; Black's rook on g1 is reduced to passivity if it ever blockades from in front. White brings the king up (**Kf2–e3–d4** etc.) to escort, and the a-pawn costs Black his rook. The lesson: **behind the passed pawn**, your rook gains scope as the pawn advances; in front, it would lose squares.

## Common mistakes

- Putting your rook **in front** of a passed pawn (yours or theirs) instead of behind it.
- Defending with a **passive** rook to "hold" a pawn — passivity loses more often than the pawn would.
- In Philidor, advancing your own pawn or leaving the 3rd rank **too early**, giving the enemy king the 6th rank.
- Checking from the **short side**, letting the attacking king approach your rook and shield itself.
- Trying Lucena with a **rook-pawn (a/h)** — the king gets boxed in the corner; that ending is often only a draw.
- Trading into a "won" R+P ending without checking it is actually Lucena and not Philidor.

## Train it

- **Lucena drill:** set up the canonical position (king in front, pawn on 7th) and win it vs an engine **10 times** until the bridge is automatic.
- **Philidor drill:** defend the 3rd-rank position vs an engine **10 times**; practice the moment to switch to rear checks.
- **Activity drill:** play out "rook behind the passed pawn" positions from both sides, **5 each**.
- **Puzzles:** Lichess endgame puzzle theme **"rookEndgame"** and **"endgame"**; aim for 15 a day.
- **Books:** Silman, *Complete Endgame Course* (rook chapters by rating); Dvoretsky, *Endgame Manual* (the "must-know" rook positions); de la Villa, *100 Endgames You Must Know*.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 1K1k4/1P6/8/8/8/8/6r1/2R5 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Win using the Lucena method.
2. **FEN:** 8/8/8/8/8/4k3/4p3/4K2r w - - 0 1 — White to move. Find the only drawing idea (frontal defense).
3. **FEN:** 8/5k2/8/8/8/4r3/4P3/4K3 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Is this Philidor-drawn? State the defensive plan for Black.
4. **FEN:** 8/8/8/8/3P4/8/r5K1/3R4 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Where does each rook belong, and what is White's plan?
5. **FEN:** 6k1/R7/8/8/8/8/5ppp/6K1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Use the 7th rank actively to hold or win.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Rc4!** building the bridge. After ...Rg1 (or side checks) the king goes **Ka7/Kc7** toward freedom; when the rook checks from the side, **interpose the rook on the 4th rank** (e.g. Rc4 shields on c-something) and the b-pawn queens. King walks out, rook blocks the check.
2. **1.Re... ** no — the king is in front of the pawn (e3/e2). White draws with the **frontal/back-rank defense**: keep the king on **e1** and check or shuffle so the black king cannot shoulder yours away; the lone e-pawn with the defending king in front cannot promote against correct rook play. The drawing idea: keep your king in front of the pawn and avoid being driven off.
3. **Yes, Philidor-drawn.** Black keeps the rook on the **3rd rank** (...Re3 along the 3rd) so White's king can't reach the 6th. When White plays **e3** (pawn to the 3rd), Black switches to **...Re1+/...Rank-1 rear checks** from behind; the exposed king cannot escape the checks. Draw.
4. White's rook belongs **behind** the d-pawn — but here Black's rook is already behind on a2 harassing; White should support the passer and **activate**: push d5–d6 with the rook ideally shifting behind it, king escorting. Black's rook is well placed behind White's pawn (on the d-file would be ideal); the side **behind** the pawn is better off. Plan: advance the pawn only with king + rook support, keep the rook active.
5. **1.Rxg7+! ** is not needed — the **rook on the 7th** is dominant. White uses checks and the active rook on the 7th rank to harass the king and pick up the f/g/h pawns or force perpetual; the active 7th-rank rook is worth at least the pawn deficit. Keep the rook on the 7th, never go passive.

</details>

## Related

- [King and Pawn Endgames](king-pawn-endgames.md)
- [Opposition](opposition.md)
- [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md)
- [Passed Pawns](passed-pawns.md)

[[lucena]] [[philidor]] [[rook-endgames]] [[seventh-rank]] [[rook-activity]] [[passed-pawns]]
